epigraph.text: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven—a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted.
epigraph.quote: It is useful to solve differential equations.

content.text:
How fast is a stone falling at the very instant it strikes the ground? How 
quickly is a population growing *right now*, not over the last decade but 
at this precise moment? These questions—deceptively simple to 
ask—launched one of the greatest intellectual revolutions in history. To 
answer them, we need a concept that does not yet exist in our mathematical 
vocabulary: the *limit*. But before we build that powerful tool, let us 
start with something entirely familiar.

You have been computing rates of change since algebra. When you divide the 
distance traveled by the time elapsed, you get an average speed. When you 
compute the slope of a line through two points, you measure how fast $y$ 
changes relative to $x$. The idea is the same every time: *how much 
does the output change per unit of input?*

content.title: Average Rate of Change
content.content:
Let $f$ be a function defined on an interval containing $a$ and $b$ with 
$a \neq b$. The **average rate of change** 
of $f$ from $a$ to $b$ is
\[
 \text{average rate of change} 
 \;=\; \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}
 \;=\; \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x},
\]
where $\Delta y = f(b) - f(a)$ denotes the change in the output and 
$\Delta x = b - a$ denotes the change in the input.

content.text:
There is nothing exotic here. This is the same "rise over run" you learned 
when you first studied linear functions. The only difference is that now the 
function need not be linear, so the rate of change depends on which two 
input values you choose.

content.title: Average speed on a road trip
content.problem:
A family drives from Phoenix to San Diego. At 10:00\,am 
their odometer reads $42{,}310$ miles; at 1:00\,pm it 
reads $42{,}490$ miles. What was their average speed?
content.solution:
We have $\Delta\text{distance} = 42{,}490 - 42{,}310 = 180$ miles and 
$\Delta\text{time} = 3$ hours, so
\[
 \text{average speed} = \frac{180 \text{ mi}}{3 \text{ hr}} = 60 \text{ mi/hr}.
\]
Of course the car did not travel at exactly 60\,mph every second. It slowed 
for traffic, accelerated on the freeway, and stopped at a rest area. The 
average rate of change tells us the *overall* rate, smoothing out all 
the variation.

content.title: Average rate of change of a quadratic
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2}$. Find the average rate of change of $f$ on each interval.

1. From $x = 1$ to $x = 3$

2. From $x = 1$ to $x = 2$

3. From $x = 1$ to $x = 1.5$

content.title: Average rate of change from a table
content.problem:
The table below records the temperature $T$ (in degrees Fahrenheit) at 
various times $t$ (in hours after midnight) on a January morning.

| $t$ (hours) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| $T(t)$ ( F) | 38 | 35 | 31 | 28 | 33 | 42 |

 Find the average rate of change of temperature from $t=0$ to 
$t=6$, and from $t=6$ to $t=10$.
content.solution:
From $t=0$ to $t=6$:
\[
 \frac{T(6)-T(0)}{6-0} = \frac{28-38}{6} = \frac{-10}{6} 
 \approx -1.67\;{}^{\circ}\text{F/hr}.
\]
The negative sign tells us the temperature was *falling*—on average, 
about $1.67$ degrees per hour.

From $t=6$ to $t=10$:
\[
 \frac{T(10)-T(6)}{10-6} = \frac{42-28}{4} = \frac{14}{4} 
 = 3.5\;{}^{\circ}\text{F/hr}.
\]
After sunrise the temperature rose at an average rate of $3.5$ degrees per 
hour.

content.text: The Secant Line: A Geometric Picture

Now let us look at what the average rate of change means 
*geometrically*. Consider the graph of a function $y = f(x)$ and two 
points on it: $P = \bigl(a,\, f(a)\bigr)$ and $Q = \bigl(b,\, f(b)\bigr)$. 
The line through $P$ and $Q$ is called a **secant line**.

content.title: Secant Line
content.content:
A **secant line** to the graph of $f$ is a line 
passing through two distinct points $\bigl(a, f(a)\bigr)$ and 
$\bigl(b, f(b)\bigr)$ on the graph. Its slope is the average rate of 
change of $f$ from $a$ to $b$:
\[
 m_{\mathrm{sec}} 
 = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.
\]

content.text:
Notice: this is not a new formula. It is the *same* quotient from 
Definition [def:average-rate], now recognized as the slope of a line. 
Every time you have computed an average rate of change, you have been 
computing the slope of a secant line—you simply did not call it that.

content.title: Equation of a secant line
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Find the equation of the secant line through the 
points where $x = 1$ and $x = 4$.
content.solution:
The two points are $P = (1, 1)$ and $Q = (4, 2)$. The slope is
\[
 m_{\mathrm{sec}} = \frac{2 - 1}{4 - 1} = \frac{1}{3}.
\]
Using the point-slope form with the point $(1, 1)$:
\[
 y - 1 = \tfrac{1}{3}(x - 1)
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 y = \tfrac{1}{3}x + \tfrac{2}{3}.
\]

content.text:
A secant line captures the average behavior of $f$ between two points. But 
what if we want to know how $f$ is behaving at a *single* point? What 
is the slope of the curve *right there*?

content.title: Archimedes of Syracuse (c.\,287--212 BC)
content.content:
The Greek mathematician Archimedes found tangent lines to spirals more than 
two thousand years ago using an ingenious method of exhaustion. His 
approach—approximating a curved figure with ever-finer straight-line 
segments—anticipates the limit process we develop here. Archimedes did 
not have the formal language of limits, but his geometric intuition was 
remarkably close to the truth that Newton and Leibniz would formalize 
centuries later.

content.text:
To find the slope of a curve at a single point $P$, we take a second 
point $Q$ on the curve and draw the secant line $PQ$. Then we slide $Q$ 
closer and closer to $P$. As $Q$ approaches $P$, the secant line rotates 
and—if all goes well—settles into a fixed position. That limiting 
position is the **tangent line** at $P$.

content.title: Tangent Line (Informal)
content.content:
The **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the 
point $P = \bigl(a, f(a)\bigr)$ is the line through $P$ whose slope is
\[
 m_{\mathrm{tan}} 
 = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a},
\]
provided this limit exists. Equivalently, writing $x = a + h$,
\[
 m_{\mathrm{tan}} 
 = \limtozero{h}\, \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}.
\]
We will make the word "limit" precise in Section [sec:limit-of-function]. 
For now, interpret it as: *the value that the expression approaches as 
$h$ gets arbitrarily close to $0$*.

content.text: Let us see this in action with a concrete calculation.

content.title: Tangent slope by shrinking the interval
content.problem:
Estimate the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point 
$(1,1)$ by computing secant slopes for smaller and smaller values of $h$.
content.solution:
We compute
\[
 \frac{f(1+h) - f(1)}{h} 
 = \frac{(1+h)^{2} - 1}{h}
 = \frac{1 + 2h + h^{2} - 1}{h}
 = \frac{2h + h^{2}}{h}
 = 2 + h.
\]

| $h$ | $\dfrac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}$ | Secant slope |
| $1$ | $2 + 1$ | $3.000$ |
| $0.5$ | $2 + 0.5$ | $2.500$ |
| $0.1$ | $2 + 0.1$ | $2.100$ |
| $0.01$ | $2 + 0.01$ | $2.010$ |
| $0.001$ | $2 + 0.001$ | $2.001$ |
| $-0.5$ | $2 + (-0.5)$ | $1.500$ |
| $-0.1$ | $2 + (-0.1)$ | $1.900$ |
| $-0.01$ | $2 + (-0.01)$ | $1.990$ |

As $h \to 0$ from *both* sides, the secant slopes approach $2$. Moreover, 
the simplified expression $2 + h$ makes the pattern undeniable: as $h$ 
becomes $0$, the expression becomes $2$. We conclude
\[
 m_{\mathrm{tan}} = \limtozero{h}\,(2+h) = 2.
\]
The tangent line at $(1,1)$ has slope $2$, so its equation is
\[
 y - 1 = 2(x - 1) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad y = 2x - 1.
\]

content.title: Tangent line to a cubic
content.problem: Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at the point $(2, 8)$.
content.solution:
We form the difference quotient:
\[
 \frac{f(2+h) - f(2)}{h}
 = \frac{(2+h)^{3} - 8}{h}.
\]
Expanding $(2+h)^{3} = 8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}$, we obtain
\[
 \frac{8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3} - 8}{h}
 = \frac{12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}}{h}
 = 12 + 6h + h^{2}.
\]
As $h \to 0$, this expression approaches $12$. Therefore
\[
 m_{\mathrm{tan}} = 12,
\]
and the tangent line is $y - 8 = 12(x-2)$, or equivalently $y = 12x - 16$.

content.title: The tangent is not always ``touching once''
content.content:
In elementary geometry you learned that a tangent to a circle touches the 
circle at exactly one point. For general curves, this is *not* a valid 
definition. A tangent line may cross the curve elsewhere, and some lines 
that touch a curve at a single point are not tangent lines. The correct 
definition involves the limit process above.

content.text:
The tangent problem asks: *What is the slope of a curve at a point?* 
The velocity problem asks the same question in a physical setting: 
*How fast is an object moving at a particular instant?*

Suppose an object moves along a straight line, and its position at time $t$ 
is given by a function $s(t)$. Over a time interval from $t = a$ to 
$t = a + h$, the object's displacement is $s(a+h) - s(a)$, and the elapsed 
time is $h$.

content.title: Average and Instantaneous Velocity
content.content:
The **average velocity** of an object with 
position function $s(t)$ over the interval $[a,\, a+h]$ is
\[
 v_{\mathrm{avg}} 
 = \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h}
 = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}.
\]
The **instantaneous velocity** at 
time $t = a$ is
\[
 v(a) = \limtozero{h}\, \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h},
\]
provided the limit exists.

content.text:
Compare this with Definition [def:tangent-informal]. The formulas are 
identical! The tangent problem and the velocity problem are the 
*same* mathematical problem dressed in different clothing. This 
unification is one of the great insights of calculus.

content.title: A ball thrown upward
content.problem:
A ball is thrown straight up from a bridge 48 feet above a river, with an 
initial velocity of $64$ ft/s. Neglecting air resistance, its height after 
$t$ seconds is
\[
 s(t) = -16t^{2} + 64t + 48 \quad\text{(feet)}.
\]

1. Find the average velocity over $[1, 3]$.

2. Find the average velocity over $[1, 1.5]$.

3. Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 1$.
content.solution:
**(a)** We compute $s(1) = -16 + 64 + 48 = 96$ and 
$s(3) = -16(9) + 64(3) + 48 = -144 + 192 + 48 = 96$. Therefore
\[
 v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(3)-s(1)}{3-1} = \frac{96-96}{2} = 0 \;\text{ft/s}.
\]
An average velocity of zero! The ball is at the same height at $t=1$ and 
$t=3$—it went up and came back down.

**(b)** $s(1.5) = -16(2.25) + 64(1.5) + 48 = -36 + 96 + 48 = 108$. 
So
\[
 v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(1.5)-s(1)}{0.5} = \frac{108-96}{0.5} 
 = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \;\text{ft/s}.
\]

**(c)** We compute the difference quotient:
\[
 \frac{s(1+h)-s(1)}{h}
 = \frac{\bigl[-16(1+h)^{2}+64(1+h)+48\bigr]-96}{h}.
\]
Expanding:
$$
 s(1+h) &= -16(1 + 2h + h^{2}) + 64 + 64h + 48 \\
 &= -16 - 32h - 16h^{2} + 64 + 64h + 48 \\
 &= 96 + 32h - 16h^{2}.
$$
Therefore
\[
 \frac{s(1+h)-s(1)}{h} = \frac{96 + 32h - 16h^{2} - 96}{h} 
 = \frac{32h - 16h^{2}}{h} = 32 - 16h.
\]
As $h \to 0$:
\[
 v(1) = \limtozero{h}\,(32-16h) = 32 \;\text{ft/s}.
\]
At exactly $t = 1$ second, the ball is rising at 32 ft/s.

content.title: When does the ball reach its peak?
content.problem:
Using the position function from Example [ex:ball-thrown], find the 
instant when the ball reaches its maximum height.
content.solution:
At the peak, the ball is momentarily stationary: its instantaneous velocity 
is zero. We compute the velocity at a general time $t = a$:
\[
 \frac{s(a+h)-s(a)}{h}
 = \frac{\bigl[-16(a+h)^{2}+64(a+h)+48\bigr] 
 - \bigl[-16a^{2}+64a+48\bigr]}{h}.
\]
Expanding and simplifying (the $-16a^{2}$, $64a$, and $48$ terms cancel):
\[
 = \frac{-32ah - 16h^{2} + 64h}{h} = -32a - 16h + 64.
\]
Taking the limit as $h \to 0$:
\[
 v(a) = -32a + 64.
\]
Setting $v(a) = 0$: \; $-32a + 64 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; a = 2$. The ball 
reaches its peak at $t = 2$ seconds. The maximum height is 
$s(2) = -16(4) + 64(2) + 48 = 112$ feet.

content.text:
Both the tangent problem and the velocity problem led us to the same 
expression:
\[
 \limtozero{h}\,\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}.
\]
In our examples, we simplified the fraction algebraically, cancelled the $h$ 
in the denominator, and then "set $h$ equal to zero" in what remained. But 
this informal approach conceals deep questions. What if the algebra does not 
simplify so neatly? What *exactly* do we mean by "the value the 
expression approaches"?

content.title: Computing Tangent Slopes (for now)
content.content:
To find the slope of the tangent line to $y = f(x)$ at $x = a$:

1. Form the difference quotient 
 $\displaystyle\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}$.

2. Simplify algebraically until you can cancel the $h$ in the 
 denominator.

3. Evaluate the simplified expression at $h = 0$ to obtain 
 $m_{\mathrm{tan}}$.

4. Write the tangent line: $y - f(a) = m_{\mathrm{tan}}(x - a)$.
In Section [sec:limit-of-function], we will replace step 3 with a 
rigorous definition of the limit.

content.title: Tangent to a rational function
content.problem:
Find the slope of the tangent line to $\displaystyle f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ 
at the point $\bigl(2, \tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)$.
content.solution:
The difference quotient is
\[
 \frac{f(2+h)-f(2)}{h}
 = \frac{\dfrac{1}{2+h} - \dfrac{1}{2}}{h}.
\]
Combining the fractions in the numerator:
\[
 = \frac{\;\dfrac{2 - (2+h)}{2(2+h)}\;}{h}
 = \frac{-h}{2h(2+h)}
 = \frac{-1}{2(2+h)}.
\]
As $h \to 0$:
\[
 m_{\mathrm{tan}} = \frac{-1}{2(2+0)} = -\frac{1}{4}.
\]
The tangent line at $\bigl(2,\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)$ is
\[
 y - \tfrac{1}{2} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(x-2)
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 y = -\tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.
\]

content.title:
Isaac Newton (1642--1727) and Gottfried Wilhelm 
Leibniz (1646--1716)
content.content:
The tangent problem and the velocity problem were central motivations for 
the invention of calculus. Newton, working in England, developed his 
"method of fluxions" in the mid-1660s, partly inspired by questions about 
planetary motion. Leibniz, working independently in Germany, created his 
own version of calculus in the 1670s with superior notation—including the 
$\frac{dy}{dx}$ and $\int$ symbols we still use today. Both men saw that 
the tangent problem and the area problem are *inverse* operations, a 
deep connection known as the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Chapter 6).

Newton was a devout, if unorthodox, Christian who wrote more pages of 
theology than mathematics. He saw his scientific work as revealing the 
designs of the Creator: "This most beautiful system of the sun, planets, 
and comets could only proceed from the counsel and dominion of an 
intelligent and powerful Being."

marginNotes.text:
But those who hope in the Lord will renew 
their strength. They will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and 
not grow weary.

The path of the righteous is like the 
morning sun, shining ever brighter till the full light of day.

For since the creation of the world God's 
invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been 
clearly seen, being understood from what has been made.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing 
on the shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Galileo Galilei
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the language with which 
God has written the universe.
